perturbation.original_text: Covenantor acknowledges that the unauthorized taking of any of Buyer's trade secrets is a crime under California Penal Code section 499(c) and is punishable by imprisonment in a state prison or in a county jail for a time not exceeding one year, or by a fine not exceeding five thousand dollars ($5,000), or by both such fine and such imprisonment.
perturbation.changed_text: Covenantor acknowledges that the unauthorized taking of any of Buyer's trade secrets may result in legal action.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states the criminal penalties for trade secret misappropriation under California law. The modified text weakens this statement by only mentioning potential 'legal action' and omitting the specific criminal penalties, including imprisonment and fines. This omission misrepresents the severity of trade secret theft under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Penal Code Section 499c defines the theft or misappropriation of trade secrets as a crime and specifies the penalties, including imprisonment and fines. The modified text omits these specific penalties, thereby misrepresenting the legal consequences of trade secret theft and potentially misleading the Covenantor about their rights and obligations under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, PEN 499c. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Penal Code - PEN PART 1. OF CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS [25 - 680.4] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 13. OF CRIMES AGAINST PROPERTY [450 - 593g] ( Title 13 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Larceny [484 - 502.9] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 499c. (a) As used in this section: (1) “Access” means to approach, a way or means of approaching, nearing, admittance to, including to instruct, communicate with, store information in, or retrieve information from a computer system or computer network. (2) “Article” means any object, material, device, or substance or copy thereof, including any writing, record, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype, model, photograph, micro-organism, blueprint, map, or tangible representation of a computer program or information, including both human and computer readable information and information while in transit. (3) “Benefit” means gain or advantage, or anything regarded by the beneficiary as gain or advantage, including benefit to any other person or entity in whose welfare he or she is interested. (4) “Computer system” means a machine or collection of machines, one or more of which contain computer programs and information, that performs functions, including, but not limited to, logic, arithmetic, information storage and retrieval, communications, and control. (5) “Computer network” means an interconnection of two or more computer systems. (6) “Computer program” means an ordered set of instructions or statements, and related information that, when automatically executed in actual or modified form in a computer system, causes it to perform specified functions. (7) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph or other reproduction of an article, and any note, drawing or sketch made of or from an article. (8) “Representing” means describing, depicting, containing, constituting, reflecting or recording. (9) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (A) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (B) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. (b) Every person is guilty of theft who, with intent to deprive or withhold the control of a trade secret from its owner, or with an intent to appropriate a trade secret to his or her own use or to the use of another, does any of the following: (1) Steals, takes, carries away, or uses without authorization, a trade secret. (2) Fraudulently appropriates any article representing a trade secret entrusted to him or her. (3) Having unlawfully obtained access to the article, without authority makes or causes to be made a copy of any article representing a trade secret. (4) Having obtained access to the article through a relationship of trust and confidence, without authority and in breach of the obligations created by that relationship, makes or causes to be made, directly from and in the presence of the article, a copy of any article representing a trade secret. (c) Every person who promises, offers or gives, or conspires to promise or offer to give, to any present or for
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the specific criminal penalties for trade secret misappropriation, which is a direct contradiction of California Penal Code Section 499c. The original text accurately reflects that unauthorized taking of trade secrets is a crime punishable by imprisonment or fines. By only stating 'legal action' may result, the modified text significantly weakens the severity and consequences as defined in the law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from California Penal Code 499c, defines trade secret theft as a crime, implying specific penalties which are completely absent in the changed text, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall recover all of such party's reasonable attorneys' fees, expenses, and costs, not limited to costs of suit, incurred in each and every such action, suit, or other proceeding, including any and all appeals or petitions relating thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees, expenses, and costs.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes an attorneys' fees provision stating that the prevailing party in a dispute shall recover their reasonable attorneys' fees and costs. The modified text removes this provision, mandating that each party bears its own costs regardless of the outcome of the dispute. This contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021, which allows parties to contract for attorneys' fees.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021 states that parties can agree to how attorneys' fees are handled. By changing the contract to say both parties must bear their own costs regardless, it goes against the right for the prevailing party to have their costs covered as allowed by law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, while the changed text stipulates that each party bears its own costs, regardless of who prevails. This contradicts CCP 1021, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows the agreement of the parties to dictate the measure and mode of compensation for attorneys. The modified text eliminates the possibility of such an agreement, effectively removing a right conferred by law and explicitly goes against what is described in the perturbation explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to ARTICLE 10 hereof, any suit brought hereon shall be brought in the state or federal courts sitting in Los Angeles, California, the parties hereto hereby waiving any claim or defense that such forum is not convenient or proper. Subject to ARTICLE 10 hereof, each party hereby agrees that any such court shall have in personam jurisdiction over it and consents to service of process in any manner authorized by California law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any suit brought hereon shall be subject to arbitration, to the exclusion of any state or federal court.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes that lawsuits related to the agreement must be filed in courts in Los Angeles, California and that all parties agree to the jurisdiction of California courts. By mandating arbitration only, this change removes a party's right to pursue a court claim. This may conflict with California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.2 which provides that a party may petition the court to order arbitration unless there are grounds to revoke the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.2 outlines the procedure for compelling arbitration, but also provides exceptions and considerations that a court must consider. The modified text removes the possibility of going to court, which removes the legal safeguards described in CCP § 1281.2.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1281.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 3. OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.2. On petition of a party to an arbitration agreement alleging the existence of a written agreement to arbitrate a controversy and that a party to the agreement refuses to arbitrate that controversy, the court shall order the petitioner and the respondent to arbitrate the controversy if it determines that an agreement to arbitrate the controversy exists, unless it determines that: (a) The right to compel arbitration has been waived by the petitioner; or (b) Grounds exist for rescission of the agreement. (c) A party to the arbitration agreement is also a party to a pending court action or special proceeding with a third party, arising out of the same transaction or series of related transactions and there is a possibility of conflicting rulings on a common issue of law or fact. For purposes of this section, a pending court action or special proceeding includes an action or proceeding initiated by the party refusing to arbitrate after the petit
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates lawsuits be brought in California courts. The changed text mandates arbitration only, removing the right to a court claim. The Perturbation Explanation notes this may conflict with California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281.2, as it removes a party's right to pursue a court claim. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1281.2, states that a court shall order arbitration if an agreement exists, "unless it determines that: (a) The right to compel arbitration has been waived by the petitioner; or (b) Grounds exist for rescission of the agreement." By removing the court option altogether, the changed text removes any opportunity for the safeguards outlined in CCP § 1281.2 regarding the enforcement of arbitration agreements.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered by courier or other means of personal service (including by means of a nationally recognized courier service or professional messenger service), or sent by facsimile or mailed first class, postage prepaid, by certified mail, return receipt requested, in all cases, addressed to:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests and other communications hereunder may be delivered electronically to the email addresses provided by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates specific methods for delivering notices, including courier, personal service, fax, or certified mail with return receipt. The modified text allows only electronic delivery. This conflicts with California Civil Code Section 1633.3(b), which states that if a law requires a record to be provided in writing, an electronic record satisfies the requirement only if the consumer affirmatively consents to electronic delivery and a record of such consent is retained.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1633.3(b) requires affirmative consent for electronic delivery of notices when a writing is required. The modified text eliminates the 'writing' requirement by allowing any electronic delivery, regardless of consent. This violates consumer protection law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1633.3 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2.5. ELECTRONIC TRANSACTIONS [1633.1 - 1633.17] ( Title 2.5 added by Stats. 1999, Ch. 428, Sec. 1. ) 1633.3. (a) Except as otherwise provided in subdivisions (b) and (c), this title applies to electronic records and electronic signatures relating to a transaction. (b) This title does not apply to transactions subject to any of the following laws: (1) A law governing the creation and execution of wills, codicils, or testamentary trusts. (2) Division 1 (commencing with Section 1101) of the Uniform Commercial Code, except Sections 1206 and 1306. (3) Divisions 3 (commencing with Section 3101), 4 (commencing with Section 4101), 5 (commencing with Section 5101), 8 (commencing with Section 8101), 9 (commencing with Section 9101), and 11 (commencing with Section 11101) of the Uniform Commercial Code. (4) A law that requires that specifically identifiable text or disclosures in a record or a portion of a record be separately signed, including initialed, from the record. However, this paragraph does not apply to Section 1677 or 1678 of this code or Section 1298 of the Code of Civil Procedur
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by allowing only electronic delivery of notices, contradicts California Civil Code Section 1633.3(b), which implies a requirement for affirmative consent when a law requires written communication, as indicated in the Law Explanation. The original text specifies 'in writing' and delivery via courier, fax or certified mail. The modified text eliminates these methods and mandates electronic delivery, without considering consent as potentially required by law. Snippet 1 highlights 1633.3 (a) and (b), particularly regarding exceptions. By removing the option of written notices, the contract risks non-compliance if affirmative consent isn't obtained for electronic notices, creating a legal contradiction.
